questions.question: Explain equal frame allocation and state one advantage and one disadvantage of this method.
keyPoints: Equal allocation divides total frames equally among all processes
Advantage: Simple to implement and fair - all processes get same memory
Disadvantage: Does not consider process size or priority - small processes waste frames while large ones suffer

questions.question: What is thrashing? Explain two conditions that lead to thrashing in a paging system.
keyPoints: Thrashing: excessive paging activity where CPU spends more time swapping pages than executing
Condition 1: Too few frames allocated to active processes
Condition 2: High degree of multiprogramming reducing frames per process
Result: High page fault rate, low CPU utilization

questions.question: Differentiate between local and global frame allocation. Which one provides more predictable performance and why?
keyPoints: Local: each process limited to its allocated frames only
Global: processes can compete for any available frame in the system
Local provides predictable performance because a process's behavior is not affected by other processes
Global is more flexible but can cause one process to impact another's performance

questions.question: Describe the working set model for frame allocation. How does it help in reducing page faults?
keyPoints: Working set = set of pages process has referenced in a recent time window (Δ)
OS monitors pages accessed and maintains enough frames to hold working set
If working set size increases, allocate more frames
If working set shrinks, reclaim excess frames
Goal: keep actively used pages in memory to reduce page faults

questions.question: What is the Page Fault Frequency (PFF) approach to frame allocation? Explain how it adjusts frame allocation.
keyPoints: PFF monitors rate of page faults for each process
If PFF is too HIGH (above upper threshold): allocate MORE frames to reduce faults
If PFF is too LOW (below lower threshold): reclaim EXCESS frames from process
Between thresholds: frame allocation is considered optimal
This is a feedback-based dynamic allocation mechanism

questions.question: Explain proportional frame allocation with an example. Why is it considered better than equal allocation?
keyPoints: Proportional allocation distributes frames based on process size
Formula: allocation = (process_size / total_memory) × total_frames
Example: Process A = 100KB, Process B = 200KB, 90 frames → A gets 30, B gets 60
Better than equal allocation because it considers actual memory needs
Prevents waste in small processes and shortage in large processes

questions.question: What is the relationship between degree of multiprogramming and thrashing? How does frame allocation play a role?
keyPoints: Higher multiprogramming = more processes sharing same frames
Each process gets fewer frames on average
With too few frames, processes cannot hold their working sets
Page fault rate increases dramatically → thrashing
CPU utilization drops because processes spend time in I/O
Solution: Reduce degree of multiprogramming or improve allocation strategy

questions.question: Discuss the various frame allocation strategies in detail. Compare equal allocation, proportional allocation, and priority-based allocation with respect to fairness, efficiency, and complexity.
keyPoints: Equal allocation: equal frames to all processes, simple but unfair to varied size processes
Proportional allocation: based on process size, more efficient use of memory, moderately complex
Priority allocation: gives more frames to high-priority processes, ensures critical tasks run well
Fairness: Equal is most fair but inefficient; proportional considers actual needs; priority may starve low-priority
Efficiency: Proportional best uses memory; priority best for critical systems
Complexity: Equal simplest; proportional requires size tracking; priority requires priority management
Choice depends on system goals: simplicity vs efficiency vs responsiveness

questions.question: Explain the working set model and Page Fault Frequency (PFF) techniques for frame allocation. How do they help in preventing thrashing? Compare their approaches.
keyPoints: Working Set Model: maintains pages referenced in recent window (Δ), allocates frames to hold working set
PFF: monitors page fault rate and adjusts frames based on thresholds
WS looks backward at recent references; PFF looks at current fault rate
WS predicts future needs based on past behavior; PFF reacts to current performance
Both prevent thrashing by ensuring adequate frames for active processes
WS smoother but requires tracking reference history; PFF simpler but can be reactive
Both are dynamic allocation strategies as opposed to static allocation

questions.question: A computer system has 200 frames and 4 processes with sizes: P1=100KB, P2=200KB, P3=50KB, P4=150KB. Calculate frame allocation using: (a) Equal allocation (b) Proportional allocation based on process size.
keyPoints: Total frames = 200, Total size = 500KB
(a) Equal allocation: 200/4 = 50 frames to each process
(b) Proportional allocation:
Verify total: 40+80+20+60 = 200 frames
Proportional gives more frames to larger processes

questions.question: Describe the concept of local vs global page replacement in the context of frame allocation. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each approach in terms of system performance and predictability.
keyPoints: Local replacement: process can only replace pages from its own allocated frames
Global replacement: process can replace any page in the system
Local with local allocation: fixed frames per process, predictable performance but inflexible
Global with global allocation: flexible but performance can be unpredictable
Local advantages: each process guaranteed resources, easier debugging, no interference
Global advantages: better utilization of available frames, can borrow from rich to poor
Local disadvantages: may waste frames if process doesn't need them, cannot help overloaded processes
Global disadvantages: one thrashing process can degrade entire system performance
Choice depends on whether predictability or efficiency is more important
